paper.title: A quantitative spectral analysis of 14 hypervelocity stars from the MMT survey

extraction.summary: 14 hypervelocity star candidates from the MMT HVS survey (Brown et al. 2014) reanalyzed with new NLTE spectral models. The paper provides revised spectroscopic parameters (Teff, log g, radial velocities, projected rotational velocities), evolutionary masses, ages, radii, luminosities, and spectroscopic distances (30-100 kpc, 10-15% uncertainty). All are main-sequence B-type stars (2.5-5.0 M_sun) except B711 (A-type). HVS 1,4,5,6,7,8,9,10,12,13 + B434,B485,B711,B733. The paper does not specify which individual stars are bound vs. unbound — the original MMT survey (Brown+2014) flagged ~22 as unbound and ~16 as bound. This paper's goal is to provide accurate spectroscopic distances for future Gaia DR2 kinematic studies. No Gaia source IDs listed in the paper.

method_chain.summary: 14 HVSs selected from the MMT HVS survey (Brown+2014, 38 late B-type HVSs). The 14 stars are those with HST proper motions available.
outputs: 14 HVS candidates with MMT low-resolution spectra and multi-band photometry

source_refs.context: The MMT HVS survey by Brown+2014 discovered 38 late B-type HVSs, 16 of which are considered to be bound to the Galaxy. We selected the 14 most interesting ones, that is, those stars which have proper motions measured by HST.

method_chain.summary: NLTE spectral analysis using Atlas12/Detail/Surface (ADS) hybrid LTE/NLTE approach. Grid of synthetic spectra: Teff=9000-16000 K, log g=3.0-4.6. Simultaneous fit of entire 3600-4500 Å range for Teff, log g, v_rad, v sin i. Improved treatment of hydrogen level dissolution for Balmer jump modeling.
outputs: Teff, log g, v_rad, v sin i for 14 stars

source_refs.context: Sect. 2-3: Atlas12/Detail/Surface NLTE approach, grid 9000-16000K, 3.0-4.6 dex
The quantitative analysis strategy as described by Irrgang+2014 was applied to simultaneously fit the entire spectral range

method_chain.summary: Evolutionary masses, ages, radii, luminosities, and rotation rates derived by interpolation of rotating stellar models (Georgy+2013, solar metallicity). v sin i multiplied by 4/pi as proxy for equatorial rotation velocity.
inputs: Teff, log g, v sin i from step-02
outputs: Stellar masses M, ages tau, radii R, luminosities log(L/L_sun), Omega/Omega_crit for 14 stars

source_refs.context: Sect. 4: Evolutionary masses and stellar ages via interpolation of Georgy+2013 tracks

method_chain.summary: Spectroscopic distances via SED fitting. Synthetic SEDs (Atlas12) fitted to optical (SDSS-DR14) and infrared (UKIDSS-DR9, ALLWISE) photometry. Angular diameter and E(B-V) fitted; extinction from Fitzpatrick (1999) with R_V=3.1. Distance = sqrt(L/(4*pi*F)).
inputs: SDSS DR14, UKIDSS DR9, ALLWISE photometry

source_refs.context: Sect. 5: SED fitting with SDSS/UKIDSS/ALLWISE photometry, distances 10-100 kpc

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS 1: Late B-type main sequence star from MMT HVS survey (Brown+2014). Teff~11120 K, log g~3.85, radial velocity ~830 km/s, distance ~99 kpc. Originally classified as unbound HVS by Brown+2014.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Explicitly analyzed as HVS from the MMT HVS survey (Brown+2014). Spectroscopy confirms B-type main sequence nature. Paper title and abstract state these are hypervelocity stars. The original Brown+2014 survey classified these as unbound/bound HVSs.

source_refs.context: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) travel so fast that they may leave the Galaxy
the systematic survey covering 12,000 square degrees of the sky led to the discovery of almost two dozen unbound HVSs of late B type
Table: HVS 1 (SDSS J090744.99+024506.9) with full parameters
We reanalyzed low-resolution optical spectra of 14 HVSs from the MMT HVS survey

citation_context_refs.context: Brown+2014 discovered 38 late B-type HVSs, 16 considered bound

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS 4: Late B-type main sequence star, Teff~13280 K, log g~3.89, radial velocity ~605 km/s, distance ~78 kpc. From MMT HVS survey.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Explicitly part of the MMT HVS survey sample. Spectroscopic reanalysis confirms main sequence nature.

source_refs.context: We reanalyzed low-resolution optical spectra of 14 HVSs from the MMT HVS survey

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS 5: Late B-type, Teff~12530 K, log g~4.20, radial velocity ~543 km/s, distance ~31 kpc. MMT HVS survey.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Explicitly part of the MMT HVS survey sample.

source_refs.context: 14 HVSs from the MMT HVS survey

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS 6: Late B-type, Teff~12190 K, log g~4.19, radial velocity ~619 km/s, distance ~58 kpc. MMT HVS survey.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Explicitly part of the MMT HVS survey sample.

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS 7: Late B-type, Teff~12500 K, log g~3.93, radial velocity ~524 km/s, distance ~48 kpc. MMT HVS survey. Consistent with previous high-resolution analysis (Przybilla+2008).
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Explicitly part of MMT HVS survey, confirmed by independent high-resolution spectroscopy.

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS 8: Late B-type, Teff~10960 K, log g~4.04, radial velocity ~500 km/s, v sin i ~282 km/s, distance ~37 kpc. MMT HVS survey. Consistent with previous high-resolution analysis (Lopez-Morales+2008).
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Explicitly part of MMT HVS survey.

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS 9: Late B-type, Teff~12030 K, log g~3.91, radial velocity ~622 km/s, v sin i ~261 km/s, distance ~67 kpc. MMT HVS survey.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Explicitly part of MMT HVS survey.

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS 10: Late B-type, Teff~11040 K, log g~4.18, radial velocity ~462 km/s, distance ~54 kpc. MMT HVS survey.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Explicitly part of MMT HVS survey.

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS 12: Late B-type, Teff~11170 K, log g~4.34, radial velocity ~545 km/s, v sin i consistent with zero, distance ~52 kpc. MMT HVS survey. Low rotation could suggest pole-on orientation.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Explicitly part of MMT HVS survey.

inclusion_assessment.summary: HVS 13: Late B-type, Teff~11370 K, log g~4.11, radial velocity ~568 km/s, distance ~95 kpc. MMT HVS survey.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Explicitly part of MMT HVS survey.

inclusion_assessment.summary: B434: Late B-type, Teff~10190 K, log g~3.85, radial velocity ~446 km/s, distance ~41 kpc. MMT HVS survey. 'B' prefix indicates a bound HVS candidate from Brown+2014.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Explicitly part of MMT HVS survey. 'B' prefix from Brown+2014 originally indicated bound status.

inclusion_assessment.summary: B485: Late B-type, Teff~15200 K (hottest in sample), log g~3.89, radial velocity ~423 km/s, distance ~33 kpc. MMT HVS survey. Highest mass in sample (4.8 M_sun).
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Explicitly part of MMT HVS survey. 'B' prefix from Brown+2014.

inclusion_assessment.summary: B711: Reclassified as early A-type star (not late B-type), Teff~9170 K, log g~3.71, radial velocity ~271 km/s, distance ~29 kpc. MMT HVS survey. Only A-type star in sample; analysis used Atlas12/Synthe LTE code.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Explicitly part of MMT HVS survey. 'B' prefix from Brown+2014.

inclusion_assessment.summary: B733: Late B-type, Teff~10750 K, log g~4.23, radial velocity ~351 km/s, distance ~10 kpc (closest in sample). MMT HVS survey. Has significant Gaia DR2 parallax but authors doubt reliability.
inclusion_assessment.confidence_reason: Explicitly part of MMT HVS survey. 'B' prefix from Brown+2014.

candidate_groups_considered.description: The full MMT HVS survey sample of 38 late B-type HVSs (Brown+2014), from which 14 were selected for this spectroscopic reanalysis. The 14 selected are those with HST proper motions available.
candidate_groups_considered.reason: 14 of 38 stars are explicitly reanalyzed in this paper. The 14 selected (HVS 1,4,5,6,7,8,9,10,12,13 and B434,B485,B711,B733) are those with HST proper motions. The remaining 24 from the original survey are not analyzed in this paper.

source_refs.context: The MMT HVS survey by Brown+2014 discovered 38 late B-type HVSs, 16 of which are considered to be bound to the Galaxy. We selected the 14 most interesting ones, that is, those stars which have proper motions measured by HST.
